learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sorting, Bucket Sort, Math, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine several cities located along a highway. After arranging them by distance, you want to find the largest empty stretch between any two neighboring cities.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine several cities located along a highway. After arranging them by distance, you want to find the largest empty stretch between any two neighboring cities.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use Bucket Sort whenever a problem asks for maximum adjacent difference after sorting while requiring O(n) time.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks for the maximum adjacent difference after sorting with an O(n) requirement, think of Bucket Sort and the Pigeonhole Principle.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return the maximum difference between two successive elements in its sorted form. If the array contains fewer than two elements, return 0. You must write an algorithm that runs in linear time and uses linear extra space.

examples.explanation: Sorted array is [1,3,6,9]. Maximum adjacent gap is 3.
Only one element exists, so no gap is possible.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Sort the array and compute the maximum adjacent difference.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of sorting the entire array, divide the value range into buckets. According to the Pigeonhole Principle, the maximum gap must occur between buckets rather than inside a bucket.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The maximum adjacent gap after sorting must occur between two neighboring non-empty buckets.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks for the maximum adjacent difference after sorting with an O(n) requirement, think of Bucket Sort and the Pigeonhole Principle.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each number is inserted into exactly one bucket. Since values inside one bucket cannot produce the maximum gap, only adjacent non-empty buckets need to be compared, giving O(n) complexity.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks for the maximum adjacent difference after sorting with an O(n) requirement, think of Bucket Sort and the Pigeonhole Principle.
commonWrongPaths: Comparing values inside the same bucket.

hints: Finding the sorted order directly is unnecessary.
Compute bucket size using min and max values.
commonMisconceptions: Comparing values inside the same bucket.

pattern.name: Pigeonhole Principle / Bucket Sort
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks for the maximum adjacent difference after sorting with an O(n) requirement, think of Bucket Sort and the Pigeonhole Principle.

approaches.title: Sort and Scan
approaches.idea: Sort the array and compute the maximum adjacent difference.

approaches.title: Radix Sort
approaches.idea: Sort the numbers using Radix Sort and then compute the maximum adjacent difference.

approaches.title: Bucket Sort using Pigeonhole Principle
approaches.idea: Find the minimum and maximum values. Compute the bucket size and create buckets storing only their minimum and maximum values. The maximum gap can only exist between consecutive non-empty buckets.
approaches.intuition: Each number is inserted into exactly one bucket. Since values inside one bucket cannot produce the maximum gap, only adjacent non-empty buckets need to be compared, giving O(n) complexity.
algorithm: If array size is less than 2, return 0.
Compute bucket size and bucket count.
Create buckets storing minimum and maximum values.
Place every number into its bucket.
Compute the gap between the current bucket minimum and previous bucket maximum.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[3,6,9,1]
Min=1 Max=9
Buckets:
[1]
[3]
[6]
[9]
Gaps=2,3,3
Answer=3

code.python:
def maximumGap(nums):
    if len(nums) < 2:
        return 0

    mn, mx = min(nums), max(nums)
    if mn == mx:
        return 0

    n = len(nums)
    bucketSize = max(1, (mx - mn) // (n - 1))
    bucketCount = (mx - mn) // bucketSize + 1

    bucketMin = [float('inf')] * bucketCount
    bucketMax = [float('-inf')] * bucketCount
    used = [False] * bucketCount

    for num in nums:
        idx = (num - mn) // bucketSize
        bucketMin[idx] = min(bucketMin[idx], num)
        bucketMax[idx] = max(bucketMax[idx], num)
        used[idx] = True

    prev = mn
    ans = 0

    for i in range(bucketCount):
        if not used[i]:
            continue
        ans = max(ans, bucketMin[i] - prev)
        prev = bucketMax[i]

    return ans
code.javascript: JavaScript requires implementing the same bucket logic using arrays for bucketMin, bucketMax, and used. The algorithm remains identical to the Java solution.

visualization.description: Distribute numbers into buckets and compare neighboring non-empty buckets.

revisionNotes: Store only bucket minimum and maximum.

interviewFollowUps: Why can't the maximum gap exist inside one bucket?
How does the Pigeonhole Principle apply?
Can Radix Sort solve this problem?
How would you return the pair producing the maximum gap?
interviewTips: Explain why O(n log n) is insufficient.
Explain why the maximum gap is between buckets.
State O(n) time and O(n) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The maximum adjacent gap after sorting must occur between two neighboring non-empty buckets.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Find Min/Max → Create Buckets → Store Min & Max → Compare Adjacent Buckets.

importantPoints: Maximum gap is never inside a bucket.
Store only min and max per bucket.
commonMistakes: Comparing values inside the same bucket.

relatedProblems: Kth Largest Element in an Array
